Math4201Exam reviewsMath 4201 Final Exam Review

Math 4201 Final Exam Review

Note

This is a review for definitions we covered in the classes. It may serve as a cheat sheet for the exam if you are allowed to use it.

Topological space

Basic definitions

Definition for topological space

A topological space is a pair of set XXX and a collection of subsets of XXX, denoted by T\mathcal{T}T (imitates the set of “open sets” in XXX), satisfying the following axioms:

  1. ∅∈T\emptyset \in \mathcal{T}∅∈T and X∈TX \in \mathcal{T}X∈T
  2. T\mathcal{T}T is closed with respect to arbitrary unions. This means, for any collection of open sets {Uα}α∈I\{U_\alpha\}_{\alpha \in I}{Uα​}α∈I​, we have ⋃α∈IUα∈T\bigcup_{\alpha \in I} U_\alpha \in \mathcal{T}⋃α∈I​Uα​∈T
  3. T\mathcal{T}T is closed with respect to finite intersections. This means, for any finite collection of open sets {U1,U2,…,Un}\{U_1, U_2, \ldots, U_n\}{U1​,U2​,…,Un​}, we have ⋂i=1nUi∈T\bigcap_{i=1}^n U_i \in \mathcal{T}⋂i=1n​Ui​∈T

Definition of open set

U⊆XU\subseteq XU⊆X is an open set if U∈TU\in \mathcal{T}U∈T

Definition of closed set

Z⊆XZ\subseteq XZ⊆X is a closed set if X∖Z∈TX\setminus Z\in \mathcal{T}X∖Z∈T

Warning

A set is closed is not the same as its not open.

In all topologies over non-empty sets, X,∅X, \emptysetX,∅ are both closed and open.

Basis

Definition of topological basis

For a set XXX, a topology basis, denoted by B\mathcal{B}B, is a collection of subsets of XXX, such that the following properties are satisfied:

  1. For any x∈Xx \in Xx∈X, there exists a B∈BB \in \mathcal{B}B∈B such that x∈Bx \in Bx∈B (basis covers the whole space)
  2. If B1,B2∈BB_1, B_2 \in \mathcal{B}B1​,B2​∈B and x∈B1∩B2x \in B_1 \cap B_2x∈B1​∩B2​, then there exists a B3∈BB_3 \in \mathcal{B}B3​∈B such that x∈B3⊆B1∩B2x \in B_3 \subseteq B_1 \cap B_2x∈B3​⊆B1​∩B2​ (every non-empty intersection of basis elements are also covered by a basis element)

Definition of topology generated by basis

Let B\mathcal{B}B be a basis for a topology on a set XXX. Then the topology generated by B\mathcal{B}B is defined by the set as follows:

TB≔{U⊆X∣∀x∈U,∃B∈B such that x∈B⊆U}\mathcal{T}_{\mathcal{B}} \coloneqq \{ U \subseteq X \mid \forall x\in U, \exists B\in \mathcal{B} \text{ such that } x\in B\subseteq U \}TB​:={U⊆X∣∀x∈U,∃B∈B such that x∈B⊆U}

This is basically a closure of B\mathcal{B}B under arbitrary unions and finite intersections

Lemma of topology generated by basis

U∈TB  ⟺  ∃{Bα}α∈I⊆BU\in \mathcal{T}_{\mathcal{B}}\iff \exists \{B_\alpha\}_{\alpha \in I}\subseteq \mathcal{B}U∈TB​⟺∃{Bα​}α∈I​⊆B such that U=⋃α∈IBαU=\bigcup_{\alpha \in I} B_\alphaU=⋃α∈I​Bα​

Definition of basis generated from a topology

Let (X,T)(X, \mathcal{T})(X,T) be a topological space. Then the basis generated from a topology is C⊆B\mathcal{C}\subseteq \mathcal{B}C⊆B such that ∀U∈T\forall U\in \mathcal{T}∀U∈T, ∀x∈U\forall x\in U∀x∈U, ∃B∈C\exists B\in \mathcal{C}∃B∈C such that x∈B⊆Ux\in B\subseteq Ux∈B⊆U.

Definition of subbasis of topology

A subbasis of a topology is a collection S⊆T\mathcal{S}\subseteq \mathcal{T}S⊆T such that ⋃U∈SU=X\bigcup_{U\in \mathcal{S}} U=X⋃U∈S​U=X.

Definition of topology generated by subbasis

Let S⊆T\mathcal{S}\subseteq \mathcal{T}S⊆T be a subbasis of a topology on XXX, then the basis generated by such subbasis is the closure of finite intersection of S\mathcal{S}S

BS≔{B∣B is the intersection of a finite number of elements of S}\mathcal{B}_{\mathcal{S}} \coloneqq \{B\mid B\text{ is the intersection of a finite number of elements of }\mathcal{S}\}BS​:={B∣B is the intersection of a finite number of elements of S}

Then the topology generated by BS\mathcal{B}_{\mathcal{S}}BS​ is the subbasis topology denoted by TS\mathcal{T}_{\mathcal{S}}TS​.

Note that all open set with respect to TS\mathcal{T}_{\mathcal{S}}TS​ can be written as a union of finitely intersections of elements of S\mathcal{S}S

Comparing topologies

Definition of finer and coarser topology

Let (X,T)(X,\mathcal{T})(X,T) and (X,T′)(X,\mathcal{T}')(X,T′) be topological spaces. Then T\mathcal{T}T is finer than T′\mathcal{T}'T′ if T′⊆T\mathcal{T}'\subseteq \mathcal{T}T′⊆T. T\mathcal{T}T is coarser than T′\mathcal{T}'T′ if T⊆T′\mathcal{T}\subseteq \mathcal{T}'T⊆T′.

Lemma of comparing basis

Let (X,T)(X,\mathcal{T})(X,T) and (X,T′)(X,\mathcal{T}')(X,T′) be topological spaces with basis B\mathcal{B}B and B′\mathcal{B}'B′. Then T\mathcal{T}T is finer than T′\mathcal{T}'T′ if and only if for any x∈Xx\in Xx∈X, x∈B′x\in B'x∈B′, B′∈B′B'\in \mathcal{B}'B′∈B′, there exists B∈BB\in \mathcal{B}B∈B, such that x∈Bx\in Bx∈B and x∈B⊆B′x\in B\subseteq B'x∈B⊆B′.

Product space

Definition of cartesian product

Let X,YX,YX,Y be sets. The cartesian product of XXX and YYY is the set of all ordered pairs (x,y)(x,y)(x,y) where x∈Xx\in Xx∈X and y∈Yy\in Yy∈Y, denoted by X×YX\times YX×Y.

Definition of product topology

Let (X,TX)(X,\mathcal{T}_X)(X,TX​) and (Y,TY)(Y,\mathcal{T}_Y)(Y,TY​) be topological spaces. Then the product topology on X×YX\times YX×Y is the topology generated by the basis

BX×Y={U×V,U∈TX,V∈TY}\mathcal{B}_{X\times Y}=\{U\times V, U\in \mathcal{T}_X, V\in \mathcal{T}_Y\}BX×Y​={U×V,U∈TX​,V∈TY​}

or equivalently,

BX×Y′={U×V,U∈BX,V∈BY}\mathcal{B}_{X\times Y}'=\{U\times V, U\in \mathcal{B}_X, V\in \mathcal{B}_Y\}BX×Y′​={U×V,U∈BX​,V∈BY​}

Product topology generated from open sets of XXX and YYY is the same as product topology generated from their corresponding basis

Subspace topology

Definition of subspace topology

Let (X,T)(X,\mathcal{T})(X,T) be a topological space and Y⊆XY\subseteq XY⊆X. Then the subspace topology on YYY is the topology given by

TY={U∩Y∣U∈T}\mathcal{T}_Y=\{U\cap Y|U\in \mathcal{T}\}TY​={U∩Y∣U∈T}

or equivalently, let B\mathcal{B}B be the basis for (X,T)(X,\mathcal{T})(X,T). Then the subspace topology on YYY is the topology generated by the basis

BY={U∩Y∣U∈B}\mathcal{B}_Y=\{U\cap Y| U\in \mathcal{B}\}BY​={U∩Y∣U∈B}

Lemma of open sets in subspace topology

Let (X,T)(X,\mathcal{T})(X,T) be a topological space and Y⊆XY\subseteq XY⊆X. Then if U⊆YU\subseteq YU⊆Y, UUU is open in (Y,TY)(Y,\mathcal{T}_Y)(Y,TY​), then UUU is open in (X,T)(X,\mathcal{T})(X,T).

This also holds for closed set in closed subspace topology

Interior and closure

Definition of interior

The interior of AAA is the largest open subset of AAA.

A∘=⋃U⊆A,U is open in XUA^\circ=\bigcup_{U\subseteq A, U\text{ is open in }X} UA∘=U⊆A,U is open in X⋃​U

Definition of closure

The closure of AAA is the smallest closed superset of AAA.

A‾=⋂U⊇A,U is closed in XU\overline{A}=\bigcap_{U\supseteq A, U\text{ is closed in }X} UA=U⊇A,U is closed in X⋂​U

Definition of neighborhood

A neighborhood of a point x∈Xx\in Xx∈X is an open set U∈TU\in \mathcal{T}U∈T such that x∈Ux\in Ux∈U.

Definition of limit points

A point x∈Xx\in Xx∈X is a limit point of AAA if every neighborhood of xxx contains a point in A−{x}A-\{x\}A−{x}.

We denote the set of all limits points of AAA by A′A'A′.

A‾=A∪A′\overline{A}=A\cup A'A=A∪A′

Sequences and continuous functions

Definition of convergence

Let XXX be a topological space. A sequence (xn)n∈N+(x_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}_+}(xn​)n∈N+​​ in XXX converges to x∈Xx\in Xx∈X if for any neighborhood UUU of xxx, there exists N∈N+N\in\mathbb{N}_+N∈N+​ such that ∀n≥N,xn∈U\forall n\geq N, x_n\in U∀n≥N,xn​∈U.

Definition of Hausdoorff space

A topological space (X,T)(X,\mathcal{T})(X,T) is Hausdorff if for any two distinct points x,y∈Xx,y\in Xx,y∈X, there exist open neighborhoods UUU and VVV of xxx and yyy respectively such that U∩V=∅U\cap V=\emptysetU∩V=∅.

Uniqueness of convergence in Hausdorff spaces

In a Hausdorff space, if a sequence (xn)n∈N+(x_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}_+}(xn​)n∈N+​​ converges to x∈Xx\in Xx∈X and y∈Xy\in Xy∈X, then x=yx=yx=y.

Closed singleton in Hausdorff spaces

In a Hausdorff space, if x∈Xx\in Xx∈X, then {x}\{x\}{x} is a closed set.

Definition of continuous function

Let (X,TX)(X,\mathcal{T}_X)(X,TX​) and (Y,TY)(Y,\mathcal{T}_Y)(Y,TY​) be topological spaces. A function f:X→Yf:X\to Yf:X→Y is continuous if for any open set U⊆YU\subseteq YU⊆Y, f−1(U)f^{-1}(U)f−1(U) is open in XXX.

Definition of point-wise continuity

Let (X,TX)(X,\mathcal{T}_X)(X,TX​) and (Y,TY)(Y,\mathcal{T}_Y)(Y,TY​) be topological spaces. A function f:X→Yf:X\to Yf:X→Y is point-wise continuous at x∈Xx\in Xx∈X if for every openset V⊆YV\subseteq YV⊆Y, f(x)∈Vf(x)\in Vf(x)∈V then there exists an open set U⊆XU\subseteq XU⊆X such that x∈Ux\in Ux∈U and f(U)⊆Vf(U)\subseteq Vf(U)⊆V.

Lemma of continuous functions

If f:X→Yf:X\to Yf:X→Y is point-wise continuous for all x∈Xx\in Xx∈X, then fff is continuous.

Properties of continuous functions

If f:X→Yf:X\to Yf:X→Y is continuous, then

  1. ∀A⊆Y\forall A\subseteq Y∀A⊆Y, f−1(Ac)=X∖f−1(A)f^{-1}(A^c)=X\setminus f^{-1}(A)f−1(Ac)=X∖f−1(A) (complements maps to complements)
  2. ∀Aα⊆Y,α∈I\forall A_\alpha\subseteq Y, \alpha\in I∀Aα​⊆Y,α∈I, f−1(⋃α∈IAα)=⋃α∈If−1(Aα)f^{-1}(\bigcup_{\alpha\in I} A_\alpha)=\bigcup_{\alpha\in I} f^{-1}(A_\alpha)f−1(⋃α∈I​Aα​)=⋃α∈I​f−1(Aα​)
  3. ∀Aα⊆Y,α∈I\forall A_\alpha\subseteq Y, \alpha\in I∀Aα​⊆Y,α∈I, f−1(⋂α∈IAα)=⋂α∈If−1(Aα)f^{-1}(\bigcap_{\alpha\in I} A_\alpha)=\bigcap_{\alpha\in I} f^{-1}(A_\alpha)f−1(⋂α∈I​Aα​)=⋂α∈I​f−1(Aα​)
  4. f−1(U)f^{-1}(U)f−1(U) is open in XXX for any open set U⊆YU\subseteq YU⊆Y.
  5. fff is continuous at x∈Xx\in Xx∈X.
  6. f−1(V)f^{-1}(V)f−1(V) is closed in XXX for any closed set V⊆YV\subseteq YV⊆Y.
  7. Assume B\mathcal{B}B is a basis for YYY, then f−1(B)f^{-1}(\mathcal{B})f−1(B) is open in XXX for any B∈BB\in \mathcal{B}B∈B.
  8. ∀A⊆X\forall A\subseteq X∀A⊆X, f(A)‾=f(A‾)\overline{f(A)}=f(\overline{A})f(A)​=f(A)

Definition of homeomorphism

Let (X,TX)(X,\mathcal{T}_X)(X,TX​) and (Y,TY)(Y,\mathcal{T}_Y)(Y,TY​) be topological spaces. A function f:X→Yf:X\to Yf:X→Y is a homeomorphism if fff is continuous, bijective and f−1:Y→Xf^{-1}:Y\to Xf−1:Y→X is continuous.

Ways to construct continuous functions

  1. If f:X→Yf:X\to Yf:X→Y is constant function, f(x)=y0f(x)=y_0f(x)=y0​ for all x∈Xx\in Xx∈X, then fff is continuous. (constant functions are continuous)
  2. If AAA is a subspace of XXX, f:A→Xf:A\to Xf:A→X is the inclusion map f(x)=xf(x)=xf(x)=x for all x∈Ax\in Ax∈A, then fff is continuous. (inclusion maps are continuous)
  3. If f:X→Yf:X\to Yf:X→Y is continuous, g:Y→Zg:Y\to Zg:Y→Z is continuous, then g∘f:X→Zg\circ f:X\to Zg∘f:X→Z is continuous. (composition of continuous functions is continuous)
  4. If f:X→Yf:X\to Yf:X→Y is continuous, AAA is a subspace of XXX, then f∣A:X→Yf|_A:X\to Yf∣A​:X→Y is continuous. (domain restriction is continuous)
  5. If f:X→Yf:X\to Yf:X→Y is continuous, ZZZ is a subspace of YYY, then f:X→Zf:X\to Zf:X→Z, g(x)=f(x)∩Zg(x)=f(x)\cap Zg(x)=f(x)∩Z is continuous. If YYY is a subspace of ZZZ, then h:X→Zh:X\to Zh:X→Z, h(x)=f(x)h(x)=f(x)h(x)=f(x) is continuous (composition of fff and inclusion map).
  6. If f:X→Yf:X\to Yf:X→Y is continuous, XXX can be written as a union of open sets {Uα}α∈I\{U_\alpha\}_{\alpha\in I}{Uα​}α∈I​, then f∣Uα:X→Yf|_{U_\alpha}:X\to Yf∣Uα​​:X→Y is continuous.
  7. If X=Z1∪Z2X=Z_1\cup Z_2X=Z1​∪Z2​, and Z1,Z2Z_1,Z_2Z1​,Z2​ are closed equipped with subspace topology, let g1:Z1→Yg_1:Z_1\to Yg1​:Z1​→Y and g2:Z2→Yg_2:Z_2\to Yg2​:Z2​→Y be continuous, and for all x∈Z1∩Z2x\in Z_1\cap Z_2x∈Z1​∩Z2​, g1(x)=g2(x)g_1(x)=g_2(x)g1​(x)=g2​(x), then f:X→Yf:X\to Yf:X→Y by f(x){g1(x),x∈Z1g2(x),x∈Z2f(x)\begin{cases}g_1(x), & x\in Z_1 \\ g_2(x), & x\in Z_2\end{cases}f(x){g1​(x),g2​(x),​x∈Z1​x∈Z2​​ is continuous. (pasting lemma)
  8. f:X→Yf:X\to Yf:X→Y is continuous, g:X→Zg:X\to Zg:X→Z is continuous if and only if H:X→Y×ZH:X\to Y\times ZH:X→Y×Z, where Y×ZY\times ZY×Z is equipped with the product topology, H(x)=(f(x),g(x))H(x)=(f(x),g(x))H(x)=(f(x),g(x)) is continuous. (proved in homework)

Metric spaces

Definition of metric

A metric on XXX is a function d:X×X→Rd:X\times X\to \mathbb{R}d:X×X→R such that ∀x,y∈X\forall x,y\in X∀x,y∈X,

  1. d(x,x)=0d(x,x)=0d(x,x)=0
  2. d(x,y)≥0d(x,y)\geq 0d(x,y)≥0
  3. d(x,y)=d(y,x)d(x,y)=d(y,x)d(x,y)=d(y,x)
  4. d(x,y)+d(y,z)≥d(x,z)d(x,y)+d(y,z)\geq d(x,z)d(x,y)+d(y,z)≥d(x,z)

Definition of metric ball

The metric ball Brd(x)B_r^{d}(x)Brd​(x) is the set of all points y∈Xy\in Xy∈X such that d(x,y)≤rd(x,y)\leq rd(x,y)≤r.

Definition of metric topology

Let XXX be a metric space with metric ddd. Then XXX is equipped with the metric topology generated by the metric balls Brd(x)B_r^{d}(x)Brd​(x) for r>0r>0r>0.

Definition of metrizable

A topological space (X,T)(X,\mathcal{T})(X,T) is metrizable if it is the metric topology for some metric ddd on XXX.

Hausdorff axiom for metric spaces

Every metric space is Hausdorff (take metric balls Br(x)B_r(x)Br​(x) and Br(y)B_r(y)Br​(y), r=d(x,y)2r=\frac{d(x,y)}{2}r=2d(x,y)​).

If a topology isn’t Hausdorff, then it isn’t metrizable.

Prove by triangle inequality and contradiction.

Common metrics in Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn

Euclidean metric

d(x,y)=∑i=1n(xi−yi)2d(x,y)=\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^n (x_i-y_i)^2}d(x,y)=i=1∑n​(xi​−yi​)2​

Square metric

ρ(x,y)=max⁡i=1n∣xi−yi∣\rho(x,y)=\max_{i=1}^n |x_i-y_i|ρ(x,y)=i=1maxn​∣xi​−yi​∣

Manhattan metric

m(x,y)=∑i=1n∣xi−yi∣m(x,y)=\sum_{i=1}^n |x_i-y_i|m(x,y)=i=1∑n​∣xi​−yi​∣

These metrics are equivalent.

Product topology and metric

If (X,d),(Y,d′)(X,d),(Y,d')(X,d),(Y,d′) are metric spaces, then X×YX\times YX×Y is metric space with metric d(x,y)=max⁡{d(x1,y1),d(x2,y2)}d(x,y)=\max\{d(x_1,y_1),d(x_2,y_2)\}d(x,y)=max{d(x1​,y1​),d(x2​,y2​)}.

Uniform metric

Let Rω\mathbb{R}^\omegaRω be the set of all infinite sequences of real numbers. Then d(x,y)‾=sup⁡i=1ωmin⁡{1,∣xi−yi∣}\overline{d(x,y)}=\sup_{i=1}^\omega \min\{1,|x_i-y_i|\}d(x,y)​=supi=1ω​min{1,∣xi​−yi​∣}, the uniform metric on Rω\mathbb{R}^\omegaRω is a metric.

Metric space and converging sequences

Let XXX be a topological space, A⊆XA\subseteq XA⊆X, xn→xx_n\to xxn​→x such that xn∈Ax_n\in Axn​∈A. Then x∈A‾x\in \overline{A}x∈A.

If XXX is a metric space, A⊆XA\subseteq XA⊆X, x∈A‾x\in \overline{A}x∈A, then there exists converging sequence xn→xx_n\to xxn​→x such that xn∈Ax_n\in Axn​∈A.

Metric defined for functions

Definition for bounded metric space

A metric space (Y,d)(Y,d)(Y,d) is bounded if there is M∈R≥0M\in \mathbb{R}^{\geq 0}M∈R≥0 such that for all y1,y2∈Yy_1,y_2\in Yy1​,y2​∈Y, d(y1,y2)≤Md(y_1,y_2)\leq Md(y1​,y2​)≤M.

Definition for metric defined for functions

Let XXX be a topological space and YYY be a bounded metric space, then the set of all maps, denoted by Map⁡(X,Y)\operatorname{Map}(X,Y)Map(X,Y), f:X→Y∈Map⁡(X,Y)f:X\to Y\in \operatorname{Map}(X,Y)f:X→Y∈Map(X,Y) is a metric space with metric ρ(f,g)=sup⁡x∈Xd(f(x),g(x))\rho(f,g)=\sup_{x\in X} d(f(x),g(x))ρ(f,g)=supx∈X​d(f(x),g(x)).

Space of continuous map is closed

Let (Map⁡(X,Y),ρ)(\operatorname{Map}(X,Y),\rho)(Map(X,Y),ρ) be a metric space defined above, then every continuous map is a limit point of some sequence of continuous maps.

Z={f∈Map⁡(X,Y)∣f is continuous}Z=\{f\in \operatorname{Map}(X,Y)|f\text{ is continuous}\}Z={f∈Map(X,Y)∣f is continuous}

ZZZ is closed in (Map⁡(X,Y),ρ)(\operatorname{Map}(X,Y),\rho)(Map(X,Y),ρ).

Quotient space

Quotient map

Let XXX be a topological space and X∗X^*X∗ is a set. q:X→X∗q:X\to X^*q:X→X∗ is a surjective map. Then qqq is a quotient map.

Quotient topology

Let (X,T)(X,\mathcal{T})(X,T) be a topological space and X∗X^*X∗ be a set, q:X→X∗q:X\to X^*q:X→X∗ is a surjective map. Then

T∗≔{U⊆X∗∣q−1(U)∈T}\mathcal{T}^* \coloneqq \{U\subseteq X^*\mid q^{-1}(U)\in \mathcal{T}\}T∗:={U⊆X∗∣q−1(U)∈T}

is a topology on X∗X^*X∗ called quotient topology.

That is equivalent to say that U⊆X∗U\subseteq X^*U⊆X∗ is open in X∗X^*X∗ if and only if p−1(U)⊆Xp^{-1}(U)\subseteq Xp−1(U)⊆X is open in XXX.

This is also called “strong continuity” since compared with the continuous condition, it requires if p−1(U)p^{-1}(U)p−1(U) is open in XXX, then UUU is open in X∗X^*X∗.

(X∗,T∗)(X^*,\mathcal{T}^*)(X∗,T∗) is called the quotient space of XXX by qqq.

Closed map and open map

f:X→Yf:X\to Yf:X→Y is a open map if for each open set UUU of XXX, f(U)f(U)f(U) is open in YYY; it is a closed map if for each closed set UUU of XXX, f(U)f(U)f(U) is closed in YYY.

Warning

Not all quotient map are closed or open:

  1. Example of quotient map that is not open nor closed:

Consider the projection map f:[0,1]→S1f:[0,1]\to S^1f:[0,1]→S1, this map maps open set [0,0.5)[0,0.5)[0,0.5) in [0,1][0,1][0,1] to non open map [0,π)[0,\pi)[0,π)

  1. Example of open map that is not closed:

Consider projection map f:R×R→Rf:\mathbb{R}\times \mathbb{R}\to \mathbb{R}f:R×R→R to first coordinate, this map is open but not closed, consider C≔{x×y∣xy=1}C\coloneqq\{x\times y\mid xy=1\}C:={x×y∣xy=1} This set is closed in R×R\mathbb{R}\times \mathbb{R}R×R but f(C)=R−{0}f(C)=\mathbb{R}-\{0\}f(C)=R−{0} is not closed in R\mathbb{R}R.

  1. Example of closed map that is not open:

Consider f:[0,1]∪[2,3]→[0,2]f:[0,1]\cup[2,3]\to [0,2]f:[0,1]∪[2,3]→[0,2] by taking -1 to elements in [2,3][2,3][2,3], this map is closed map but not open, since f([2,3])=[1,2]f([2,3])=[1,2]f([2,3])=[1,2] is not open in [0,2][0,2][0,2] but [2,3][2,3][2,3] is open in [0,1]∪[2,3][0,1]\cup[2,3][0,1]∪[2,3]

Equivalent classes

∼\sim∼ is a subset of X×XX\times XX×X with the following properties:

  1. x∼xx\sim xx∼x for all x∈Xx\in Xx∈X.
  2. If (x,y)∈∼(x,y)\in \sim(x,y)∈∼, then (y,x)∈∼(y,x)\in \sim(y,x)∈∼.
  3. If (x,y)∈∼(x,y)\in \sim(x,y)∈∼ and (y,z)∈∼(y,z)\in \sim(y,z)∈∼, then (x,z)∈∼(x,z)\in \sim(x,z)∈∼.

The equivalence classes of x∈Xx\in Xx∈X is denoted by [x]={y∈X∣y∼x}[x]=\{y\in X|y\sim x\}[x]={y∈X∣y∼x}.

We can use equivalent classes to define quotient space.

Theorem 22.2

Let p:X→Yp:X\to Yp:X→Y be a quotient map. Let ZZZ be a space and let g:X→Zg:X\to Zg:X→Z be a map that is constant on each set p−1({y})p^{-1}(\{y\})p−1({y}), for y∈Yy\in Yy∈Y. Then ggg induces a map f:Y→Zf:Y\to Zf:Y→Z such that f∘p=gf\circ p=gf∘p=g. The induced map fff is continuous if and only if ggg is continuous; fff is a quotient map if and only if ggg is a quotient map.

Prove by setting f(p(x))=g(x)f(p(x))=g(x)f(p(x))=g(x), then g−1(V)=p−1(f−1(V))g^{-1}(V)=p^{-1}(f^{-1}(V))g−1(V)=p−1(f−1(V)) for VVV open in ZZZ.

Connectedness and compactness of metric spaces

Connectedness and separation

Definition of separation

Let X=(X,T)X=(X,\mathcal{T})X=(X,T) be a topological space. A separation of XXX is a pair of open sets U,V∈TU,V\in \mathcal{T}U,V∈T that:

  1. U≠∅U\neq \emptysetU=∅ and V≠∅V\neq \emptysetV=∅ (that also equivalent to U≠XU\neq XU=X and V≠XV\neq XV=X)
  2. U∩V=∅U\cap V=\emptysetU∩V=∅
  3. X=U∪VX=U\cup VX=U∪V (∀x∈X\forall x\in X∀x∈X, x∈Ux\in Ux∈U or x∈Vx\in Vx∈V)

Some interesting corollary:

  • Any non-trivial (not ∅\emptyset∅ or XXX) clopen set can create a separation.
    • Proof: Let UUU be a non-trivial clopen set. Then UUU and UcU^cUc are disjoint open sets whose union is XXX.
  • For subspace Y⊂XY\subset XY⊂X, a separation of YYY is a pair of open sets U,V∈TYU,V\in \mathcal{T}_YU,V∈TY​ such that:
    1. U≠∅U\neq \emptysetU=∅ and V≠∅V\neq \emptysetV=∅ (that also equivalent to U≠YU\neq YU=Y and V≠YV\neq YV=Y)
    2. U∩V=∅U\cap V=\emptysetU∩V=∅
    3. Y=U∪VY=U\cup VY=U∪V (∀y∈Y\forall y\in Y∀y∈Y, y∈Uy\in Uy∈U or y∈Vy\in Vy∈V)
    • If A‾\overline{A}A is closure of AAA in XXX, same for B‾\overline{B}B, then the closure of AAA in YYY is A‾∩Y\overline{A}\cap YA∩Y and the closure of BBB in YYY is B‾∩Y\overline{B}\cap YB∩Y. Then for separation U,VU,VU,V of YYY, A‾∩B=A∩B‾=∅\overline{A}\cap B=A\cap \overline{B}=\emptysetA∩B=A∩B=∅.

Definition of connectedness

A topological space XXX is connected if there is no separation of XXX.

Tip

Connectedness is a local property. (That is, even the big space is connected, the subspace may not be connected. Consider R\mathbb{R}R with the usual metric. R\mathbb{R}R is connected, but R∖{0}\mathbb{R}\setminus\{0\}R∖{0} is not connected.)

Connectedness is a topological property. (That is, if XXX and YYY are homeomorphic, then XXX is connected if and only if YYY is connected. Consider if not, then separation of XXX gives a separation of YYY.)

Lemma of connected subspace

If A,BA,BA,B is a separation of a topological space XXX, and Y⊆XY\subseteq XY⊆X is a connected subspace with subspace topology, then YYY is either contained in AAA or BBB.

Easy to prove by contradiction. Try to construct a separation of YYY.

Theorem of connectedness of union of connected subsets

Let {Aα}α∈I\{A_\alpha\}_{\alpha\in I}{Aα​}α∈I​ be a collection of connected subsets of a topological space XXX such that ⋂α∈IAα\bigcap_{\alpha\in I} A_\alpha⋂α∈I​Aα​ is non-empty. Then ⋃α∈IAα\bigcup_{\alpha\in I} A_\alpha⋃α∈I​Aα​ is connected.

Easy to prove by lemma of connected subspace.

Lemma of compressing connectedness

Let A⊆XA\subseteq XA⊆X be a connected subspace of a topological space XXX and A⊆B⊆A‾A\subseteq B\subseteq \overline{A}A⊆B⊆A. Then BBB is connected.

Easy to prove by lemma of connected subspace. Suppose C,DC,DC,D is a separation of BBB, then AAA lies completely in either CCC or DDD. Without loss of generality, assume A⊆CA\subseteq CA⊆C. Then A‾⊆C‾\overline{A}\subseteq\overline{C}A⊆C and A‾∩D=∅\overline{A}\cap D=\emptysetA∩D=∅ (from C‾∩D=∅\overline{C}\cap D=\emptysetC∩D=∅ by closure of AAA). (contradiction that DDD is nonempty) So DDD is disjoint from A‾\overline{A}A, and hence from BBB. Therefore, BBB is connected.

Theorem of connected product space

Any finite cartesian product of connected spaces is connected.

Prove using the union of connected subsets theorem. Using fiber bundle like structure union with non-empty intersection.

Application of connectedness in real numbers

Real numbers are connected.

Using the least upper bound and greatest lower bound property, we can prove that any interval in real numbers is connected.

Intermediate Value Theorem

Let f:[a,b]→Rf:[a,b]\to \mathbb{R}f:[a,b]→R be continuous. If c∈Rc\in\mathbb{R}c∈R is such that f(a)<c<f(b)f(a)<c<f(b)f(a)<c<f(b), then there exists x∈[a,b]x\in [a,b]x∈[a,b] such that f(x)=cf(x)=cf(x)=c.

If false, then we can use the disjoint interval with projective map to create a separation of [a,b][a,b][a,b].

Definition of path-connected space

A topological space XXX is path-connected if for any two points x,x′∈Xx,x'\in Xx,x′∈X, there is a continuous map γ:[0,1]→X\gamma:[0,1]\to Xγ:[0,1]→X such that γ(0)=x\gamma(0)=xγ(0)=x and γ(1)=x′\gamma(1)=x'γ(1)=x′. Any such continuous map is called a path from xxx to x′x'x′.

  • Every connected space is path-connected.
    • The converse may not be true, consider the topologists’ sine curve.

Compactness

Definition of compactness via open cover and finite subcover

Let X=(X,T)X=(X,\mathcal{T})X=(X,T) be a topological space. An open cover of XXX is A⊂T\mathcal{A}\subset \mathcal{T}A⊂T such that X=⋃A∈AAX=\bigcup_{A\in \mathcal{A}} AX=⋃A∈A​A. A finite subcover of A\mathcal{A}A is a finite subset of A\mathcal{A}A that covers XXX.

XXX is compact if every open cover of XXX has a finite subcover (i.e. X=⋃A∈AA  ⟹  ∃A′⊂AX=\bigcup_{A\in \mathcal{A}} A\implies \exists \mathcal{A}'\subset \mathcal{A}X=⋃A∈A​A⟹∃A′⊂A finite such that X=⋃A∈A′AX=\bigcup_{A\in \mathcal{A}'} AX=⋃A∈A′​A).

Definition of compactness via finite intersection property

A collection {Cα}α∈I\{C_\alpha\}_{\alpha\in I}{Cα​}α∈I​ of subsets of a set XXX has finite intersection property if for every finite subcollection {Cα1,...,Cαn}\{C_{\alpha_1}, ..., C_{\alpha_n}\}{Cα1​​,...,Cαn​​} of {Cα}α∈I\{C_\alpha\}_{\alpha\in I}{Cα​}α∈I​, we have ⋂i=1nCαi≠∅\bigcap_{i=1}^n C_{\alpha_i}\neq \emptyset⋂i=1n​Cαi​​=∅.

Let X=(X,T)X=(X,\mathcal{T})X=(X,T) be a topological space. XXX is compact if every collection {Zα}α∈I\{Z_\alpha\}_{\alpha\in I}{Zα​}α∈I​ of closed subsets of XXX satisfies the finite intersection property has a non-empty intersection (i.e. ∀{Zα1,...,Zαn}⊂{Zα}α∈I,⋂i=1nZαi≠∅  ⟹  ⋂α∈IZα≠∅\forall \{Z_{\alpha_1}, ..., Z_{\alpha_n}\}\subset \{Z_\alpha\}_{\alpha\in I}, \bigcap_{i=1}^n Z_{\alpha_i} \neq \emptyset\implies \bigcap_{\alpha\in I} Z_\alpha \neq \emptyset∀{Zα1​​,...,Zαn​​}⊂{Zα​}α∈I​,⋂i=1n​Zαi​​=∅⟹⋂α∈I​Zα​=∅).

Compactness is a local property

Let XXX be a topological space. A subset Y⊆XY\subseteq XY⊆X is compact if and only if every open covering of YYY (set open in XXX) has a finite subcovering of YYY.

  • A space XXX is compact but the subspace may not be compact.
    • Consider X=[0,1]X=[0,1]X=[0,1] and Y=[0,1/2)Y=[0,1/2)Y=[0,1/2). YYY is not compact because the open cover {(0,1/n):n∈N}\{(0,1/n):n\in \mathbb{N}\}{(0,1/n):n∈N} does not have a finite subcover.
  • A compact subspace may live in a space that is not compact.
    • Consider X=RX=\mathbb{R}X=R and Y=[0,1]Y=[0,1]Y=[0,1]. YYY is compact but XXX is not compact.

Closed subspaces of compact spaces

A closed subspace of a compact space is compact.

A compact subspace of Hausdorff space is closed.

Each point not in the closed set have disjoint open neighborhoods with the closed set in Hausdorff space.

Theorem of compact subspaces with Hausdorff property

If YYY is compact subspace of a Hausdorff space XXX, x0∈X−Yx_0\in X-Yx0​∈X−Y, then there are disjoint open neighborhoods U,V⊆XU,V\subseteq XU,V⊆X such that x0∈Ux_0\in Ux0​∈U and Y⊆VY\subseteq VY⊆V.

Image of compact space under continuous map is compact

Let f:X→Yf:X\to Yf:X→Y be a continuous map and XXX is compact. Then f(X)f(X)f(X) is compact.

Tube lemma

Let X,YX,YX,Y be topological spaces and YYY is compact. Let N⊆X×YN\subseteq X\times YN⊆X×Y be an open set contains X×{y0}X\times \{y_0\}X×{y0​} for y0∈Yy_0\in Yy0​∈Y. Then there exists an open set W⊆YW\subseteq YW⊆Y is open containing y0y_0y0​ such that NNN contains X×WX\times WX×W.

Apply the finite intersection property of open sets in X×YX\times YX×Y. Projection map is continuous.

Product of compact spaces is compact

Let X,YX,YX,Y be compact spaces, then X×YX\times YX×Y is compact.

Any finite product of compact spaces is compact.

Compact subspaces of real numbers

Every closed and bounded subset of real numbers is compact

[a,b][a,b][a,b] is compact in R\mathbb{R}R with standard topology.

Good news for real numbers

Any of the three properties is equivalent for subsets of real numbers (product of real numbers):

  1. A⊆RnA\subseteq \mathbb{R}^nA⊆Rn is closed and bounded (with respect to the standard metric or spherical metric on Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn).
  2. A⊆RnA\subseteq \mathbb{R}^nA⊆Rn is compact.

Extreme value theorem

If f:X→Rf:X\to \mathbb{R}f:X→R is continuous map with XXX being compact. Then fff attains its minimum and maximum. (there exists xm,xM∈Xx_m,x_M\in Xxm​,xM​∈X such that f(xm)≤f(x)≤f(xM)f(x_m)\leq f(x)\leq f(x_M)f(xm​)≤f(x)≤f(xM​) for all x∈Xx\in Xx∈X)

Lebesgue number lemma

For a compact metric space (X,d)(X,d)(X,d) and an open covering {Uα}α∈I\{U_\alpha\}_{\alpha\in I}{Uα​}α∈I​ of XXX. Then there is δ>0\delta>0δ>0 such that for every subset A⊆XA\subseteq XA⊆X with diameter less than δ\deltaδ, there is α∈I\alpha\in Iα∈I such that A⊆UαA\subseteq U_\alphaA⊆Uα​.

Apply the extreme value theorem over the mapping of the averaging function for distance of points to the X−UαX-U_\alphaX−Uα​. Find minimum radius of balls that have some UαU_\alphaUα​ containing the ball.

Definition for uniform continuous function

fff is uniformly continuous if for any ϵ>0\epsilon > 0ϵ>0, there exists δ>0\delta > 0δ>0 such that for any x1,x2∈Xx_1,x_2\in Xx1​,x2​∈X, if d(x1,x2)<δd(x_1,x_2)<\deltad(x1​,x2​)<δ, then d(f(x1),f(x2))<ϵd(f(x_1),f(x_2))<\epsilond(f(x1​),f(x2​))<ϵ.

Uniform continuity theorem

Let f:X→Yf:X\to Yf:X→Y be a continuous map between two metric spaces. If XXX is compact, then fff is uniformly continuous.

Definition of isolated point

A point x∈Xx\in Xx∈X is an isolated point if {x}\{x\}{x} is an open subset of XXX.

Theorem of isolated point in compact spaces

Let XXX be a nonempty compact Hausdorff space. If XXX has no isolated points, then XXX is uncountable.

Proof using infinite nested closed intervals should be nonempty.

Variation of compactness

Limit point compactness

A topological space XXX is limit point compact if every infinite subset of XXX has a limit point in XXX.

  • Every compact space is limit point compact.

Sequentially compact

A topological space XXX is sequentially compact if every sequence in XXX has a convergent subsequence.

  • Every compact space is sequentially compact.

Equivalence of three in metrizable spaces

If XXX is a metrizable space, then the following are equivalent:

  1. XXX is compact.
  2. XXX is limit point compact.
  3. XXX is sequentially compact.

Local compactness

A space XXX is locally compact if every point x∈Xx\in Xx∈X, there is a compact subspace KKK of XXX containing a neighborhood UUU of xxx x∈U⊆Kx\in U\subseteq Kx∈U⊆K such that KKK is compact.

Theorem of one point compactification

Let XXX be a locally compact Hausdorff space if and only if there exists a topological space YYY satisfying the following properties:

  1. XXX is a subspace of YYY.
  2. Y−XY-XY−X has one point, usually denoted by ∞\infty∞.
  3. YYY is compact and Hausdorff.

The YYY is defined as follows:

U⊆YU\subseteq YU⊆Y is open if and only if one of the following holds.

  1. U⊆XU\subseteq XU⊆X and UUU is open in XXX
  2. ∞∈U\infty \in U∞∈U and Y−U⊆XY-U\subseteq XY−U⊆X, and Y−UY-UY−U is compact.

Countability and Separation Axioms

Countability Axioms

First countability axiom

A topological space (X,T)(X,\mathcal{T})(X,T) satisfies the first countability axiom if any point x∈Xx\in Xx∈X, there is a sequence of open neighborhoods of xxx, {Vn}n=1∞\{V_n\}_{n=1}^\infty{Vn​}n=1∞​ such that any open neighborhood UUU of xxx contains one of VnV_nVn​.

Apply the theorem above, we have if (X,T)(X,\mathcal{T})(X,T) satisfies the first countability axiom, then:

  1. Every convergent sequence converges to a point in the closure of the sequence.

Space that every convergent sequence not converges to a point in the closure of the sequence.

Consider Rω\mathbb{R}^\omegaRω with the box topology.

And A=(0,1)×(0,1)×⋯A=(0,1)\times (0,1)\times \cdotsA=(0,1)×(0,1)×⋯ and x=(0,0,⋯ )x=(0,0,\cdots)x=(0,0,⋯).

x∈A‾x\in \overline{A}x∈A but no sequence converges to xxx.

Suppose there exists such sequence, {xn=(x1n,x2n,⋯ )}n=1∞\{x_n=(x_1^n,x_2^n,\cdots)\}_{n=1}^\infty{xn​=(x1n​,x2n​,⋯)}n=1∞​.

Take B=(−x11,x11)×(−x22,x22)×⋯B=(-x_1^1,x_1^1)\times(-x_2^2,x_2^2)\times \cdotsB=(−x11​,x11​)×(−x22​,x22​)×⋯, this is basis containing xxx but none of xnx_nxn​.

  1. If f:X→Yf:X\to Yf:X→Y such that for any sequence {xn}n=1∞\{x_n\}_{n=1}^\infty{xn​}n=1∞​ in XXX, f(xn)→f(x)f(x_n)\to f(x)f(xn​)→f(x), then fff is continuous.

Second countability axiom

Let (X,T)(X,\mathcal{T})(X,T) be a topological space, then XXX satisfies the second countability axiom if XXX has a countable basis.

If XXX is second countable, then:

  1. Any discrete subspace YYY of XXX is countable
  2. There exists a countable subset of XXX that is dense in XXX.
  3. Every open covering of XXX has a countable subcover (That is if X=⋃α∈IUαX=\bigcup_{\alpha\in I} U_\alphaX=⋃α∈I​Uα​, then there exists a countable subcover {Uα1,...,Uα∞}\{U_{\alpha_1}, ..., U_{\alpha_\infty}\}{Uα1​​,...,Uα∞​​} of XXX) (also called Lindelof spaces)

Separation Axioms

Hausdorff spaces

A topological space (X,T)(X,\mathcal{T})(X,T) is Hausdorff if for any two distinct points x,y∈Xx,y\in Xx,y∈X, there are disjoint open sets U,VU,VU,V such that x∈Ux\in Ux∈U and y∈Vy\in Vy∈V. (note that U∪VU\cup VU∪V may not be XXX, compared with definition of separation)

Some corollaries:

  1. A subspace of Hausdorff space is Hausdorff, and a product of Hausdorff spaces is Hausdorff.

Regular spaces

A topological space (X,T)(X,\mathcal{T})(X,T) is regular if for any x∈Xx\in Xx∈X and any closed set A⊆XA\subseteq XA⊆X such that x∉Ax\notin Ax∈/A, there are disjoint open sets U,VU,VU,V such that x∈Ux\in Ux∈U and A⊆VA\subseteq VA⊆V.

Some corollaries:

  1. XXX is regular if and only if given a point xxx and a open neighborhood UUU of xxx, there is open neighborhood VVV of xxx such that V‾⊆U\overline{V}\subseteq UV⊆U.
  2. A subspace of regular space is regular, and a product of regular spaces is regular.

Normal spaces

A topological space (X,T)(X,\mathcal{T})(X,T) is normal if for any disjoint closed sets A,B⊆XA,B\subseteq XA,B⊆X, there are disjoint open sets U,VU,VU,V such that A⊆UA\subseteq UA⊆U and B⊆VB\subseteq VB⊆V.

Some corollaries:

  1. XXX is normal if and only if given a closed set A⊆XA\subseteq XA⊆X, there is open neighborhood VVV of AAA such that V‾⊆U\overline{V}\subseteq UV⊆U.
  2. Every compact Hausdorff spaces is normal.
Caution

Product of normal spaces may not be normal (consider Sorgenfrey plane)

Regular space with countable basis is normal

Let XXX be a regular space with countable basis, then XXX is normal.

Prove by taking disjoint open neighborhoods by countable cover.

Urysohn Lemma

Let XXX be a normal space, A,BA,BA,B be two closed disjoint set in XXX, then there exists continuous function: f:X→[0,1]f:X\to[0,1]f:X→[0,1] such that f(A)={0}f(A)=\{0\}f(A)={0} and f(B)={1}f(B)=\{1\}f(B)={1}.

Urysohn metrization theorem

If XXX is normal (regular and second countable) topological space, then XXX is metrizable.
